idea.hook: How COLD can space get? Think beyond frozen!
facts: Average deep space temperature: around -270°C (3 Kelvin).
Absolute Zero (0 Kelvin) is the theoretical limit: -273.15°C.
The Boomerang Nebula is the coldest known natural place in the ENTIRE universe.
Its temperature: a mind-numbing -272.15°C (just 1 Kelvin above absolute zero!).
It's even colder than the Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB), the 'afterglow' of the Big Bang, which is 2.7 Kelvin.
idea.payoff: This cosmic freezer, chilling itself through rapid gas expansion, holds the record for extreme cold, proving that even the vacuum of space can get far colder than expected!
idea.title: Universe's Absolute COLDEST Spot! 🧊🌌

scenes.text:
How COLD can space get?
Think beyond frozen!
scenes.visual: A dark, icy-blue space background with subtle, distant stars. A faint frost-like overlay appears briefly.
A starry field slowly zooms in. Digital temperature readings in Celsius and Kelvin subtly appear around the text.

scenes.text:
Coldest place in the UNIVERSE?
The Boomerang Nebula.
scenes.visual: An artistic, striking visual of the bipolar Boomerang Nebula (pale blue/white gas expanding).

scenes.text:
Its temp: -272.15°C (1K)!
Colder than Big Bang's afterglow (CMB: 2.7K).
scenes.visual: The Boomerang Nebula visual remains, with temperature readings overlaid, highlighting the '1K' value. A faint 'ripple' effect representing CMB appears and fades.

scenes.text:
A cosmic freezer, self-chilling via gas expansion.
Extreme cold, beyond expectation!
scenes.visual: A wide shot of the Boomerang Nebula, slowly zooming out, surrounded by dark space. Subtle, shimmering ice crystals animate briefly around the nebula's edges.
